did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLOv8 deep learning model (a state-of-the-art object detection algorithm) to detect and classify moles as benign or malignant in a skin cancer context. The system achieved 93% accuracy for detecting malignant moles and 88% accuracy for classifying them, utilizing the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and evaluating a client-server-based mobile application for skin cancer diagnosis using the YOLOv8 model. It details specific methodologies, dataset usage (ISIC), implementation steps, and reports original quantitative results including accuracy, precision, recall, and SUS usability scores. Although published in a conference proceedings (2024 Innovations in Intelligent Systems and Applications Conference), it contains full original research content rather than being a review or abstract.

title: Client-Server-Based Mobile Application for Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: 2024 Innovations in Intelligent Systems and Applications Conference (ASYU)
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated a client-server-based mobile application for the early detection and classification of benign and malignant moles to address the subjectivity and limitations of dermatologist visual examinations. The system utilizes the YOLOv8 deep learning model for real-time mole detection on Android devices and a server-based classification model to determine malignancy. The models were trained and evaluated using the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, which comprised 3,296 images of benign and malignant moles. Evaluation results indicated that the detection model achieved 93% accuracy for malignant moles and 90% for benign moles, while the classification model achieved 88% accuracy for malignant moles and 86% for benign moles. Usability testing with elderly participants yielded a high System Usability Scale (SUS) score of 92.5, confirming the application's user-friendliness. The authors conclude that the application serves as a useful tool for regular monitoring and early detection, though it is not a replacement for professional medical consultation.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as one of the two primary classes in the ISIC dataset (1800 images) and the target of detection and classification models. The detection model achieved 93% accuracy and the classification model 86% accuracy for benign moles.

Studied as one of the two primary classes in the ISIC dataset (1496 images) and the target of detection and classification models. The detection model achieved 90% accuracy and the classification model 88% accuracy for malignant moles. The paper focuses on early detection of these to prevent skin cancer progression.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and class labels (benign/malignant) for moles in images/video frames.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses YOLOv8 for real-time mole detection, outputting bounding boxes and class labels. Evaluation metrics include precision, recall, and Intersection over Union (IoU) for detecting benign and malignant moles.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant) for detected moles.
all_task_types.details: A server-based PyTorch model classifies moles as benign or malignant. The task is binary classification with reported accuracies of 86% for benign and 88% for malignant classes.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset consisting of processed images of benign and malignant moles. The images were preprocessed using Roboflow, resized to 320x320 pixels, and split into training, validation, and testing sets.

use.training: Used to train the YOLOv8 detection model and the server-based classification model.
use.validation: Used to prevent over- or under-fitting during the training of the detection model.
use.testing: Used to gather evaluation metrics (precision, recall, IoU) for the detection model.
use.other: A subset of 200 moles from the dataset (or real-time captures not explicitly split in the count but derived from the same source context) was used for specialist evaluation of false positives/negatives.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the usability and user experience of the mobile application across different user groups (medical professionals, computer engineers, and elderly individuals).

statistical_methods_used.method: Precision, Recall, and Intersection over Union (IoU)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the detection model on the testing set, specifically measuring true positive rates, false positive rates, and bounding box accuracy.
To analyze the classification performance of the server-based model, determining true positive, false positive, true negative, and false negative rates for benign and malignant classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Evaluation was based on descriptive performance metrics (precision, recall, IoU, accuracy) and System Usability Scale (SUS) scores.

ISIC_dataset_testing_set.notes: The detection model achieved a true positive rate of 93% for malignant moles and correctly identified 85% of benign cases, misclassifying 15% as malignant. It misclassified only 7% of malignant cases as benign.

ISIC_dataset_testing_set_classification.notes: The classification model correctly identified 89% of benign cases and 91% of malignant cases. It misclassified 11% of benign cases as malignant and 9% of malignant cases as benign.

Specialist_Evaluation_Set.dataset_description: 200 moles detected/classified via mobile device camera with unknown annotations
Specialist_Evaluation_Set.notes: The detection model detected 97 malignant and 103 benign moles. The classification model classified 102 malignant and 98 benign moles. A specialist manually evaluated these for false positives/negatives.

Usability_Evaluation.dataset_description: SUS Tests across three user groups
Usability_Evaluation.notes: High SUS scores indicate high usability and satisfaction across all groups. The application is not a replacement for medical consultation.

architecture_used.main_model: YOLOv8 (You Only Look Once version 8) for detection; Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented in PyTorch for classification
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; YOLOv8 is described as a state-of-the-art object detection algorithm with improved performance over previous versions, but specific pretraining datasets (e.g., COCO) are not detailed for the final model in this study.

detection_model.deployment_format: TensorFlow Lite (TFLite) for Android mobile deployment
detection_model.optimization: Converted to TFLite for real-time detection on mobile devices

classification_model.reason_for_server: Higher computational demands and to avoid accuracy loss associated with TFLite conversion

training_details.environment: Google Colab Pro with A100 GPU and 90 GB RAM
training_details.preprocessing: Resized to 320x320 pixels; auto-orientation correction; grayscale conversion; histogram equalization; contrast stretching; image isolation
training_details.split: Training: 2,324 images; Validation: 643 images; Testing: 329 images

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Faster Region Based Convolutional Neural Networks (FR-CNN)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 320x320 pixels for uniform input size and consistency with mobile camera settings
Auto-orientation correction based on metadata to handle tilted or skewed images
Image isolation to separate the mole from surrounding skin
Histogram equalization to normalize the dataset
Contrast stretching to normalize the dataset
Grayscale conversion to remove color and focus on intensity values
conclusions: The developed client-server-based mobile application effectively detects and classifies moles as benign or malignant using the YOLOv8 deep learning model and a server-side PyTorch classification model.
The detection model achieved high performance metrics on the ISIC testing set, with a precision of 0.905, recall of 0.897, and IoU of 0.962 across all classes.
The detection model demonstrated a 93% true positive rate for malignant moles and correctly identified 85% of benign cases, though it misclassified 15% of benign cases as malignant.
The classification model achieved 86% accuracy for benign moles and 88% accuracy for malignant moles on the ISIC testing set.

Specialist evaluation of 200 real-time mobile-captured moles confirmed the detection model's accuracy at 93% for benign and 90% for malignant moles, and the classification model's accuracy at 86% for benign and 88% for malignant moles.

Usability testing yielded high System Usability Scale (SUS) scores across three user groups: medical professionals (94.25), computer engineers (98), and elderly individuals over 70 (92.5), indicating the application is user-friendly for diverse demographics.

The application serves as a tool for regular monitoring and early detection, particularly beneficial for individuals with limited medical access or numerous moles, but is not a replacement for professional medical consultation.
important_remarks: The study focuses exclusively on benign vs. malignant moles, excluding other critical skin cancer types such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma from the experimental classification task.

The dataset is small (3,296 images) and derived from the ISIC dataset, which may limit the generalizability of the model to diverse real-world populations with varying skin tones and lighting conditions.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as descriptive accuracy and precision metrics.

The classification model was deployed on a server to avoid accuracy loss associated with converting to TensorFlow Lite (TFLite), creating a dependency on network connectivity and raising potential latency and privacy concerns for real-time mobile use.
The detection model shows a tendency to over-classify benign moles as malignant (15% false positive rate for benign class), which could lead to unnecessary anxiety and medical consultations.
Specialist evaluation was conducted on a small, unannotated set of 200 real-time mobile-captured images, which limits the robustness of the external validation compared to the held-out ISIC test set.
Usability testing included a very small sample size (n=35 total: 20 medical professionals, 5 engineers, 10 elderly), which may not be sufficient to represent the broader target population.
The paper does not report key training hyperparameters such as the optimizer, loss function, or learning rate, limiting reproducibility.
The application is explicitly stated to be a monitoring tool and not a replacement for professional medical diagnosis, highlighting its role as an adjunct rather than a diagnostic standard.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while the ISIC split is described, the preprocessing steps (Roboflow) and potential overlap with other public datasets are not detailed.
